us-politics.headline: US-Iran Conflict Escalates as Military Strikes Continue and Tensions Mount
us-politics.body:
The US-Iran conflict has intensified significantly, with Iran striking Gulf oil refineries and the US deploying B-52 bombers and the USS Gerald R. Ford aircraft carrier to the region. President Trump has warned that the US military has 'not even started destroying what's left in Iran,' threatening strikes on bridges and power plants. Families of slain US service members have reportedly urged Trump to 'finish the job,' while thousands of troops are being evacuated from a key Bahrain base, sending military communities scrambling.

Iran's strategic position near the Strait of Hormuz gives it significant leverage, as any disruption to the waterway could severely impact global oil supplies and further drive up US gas prices. Analysts note that Iran's ability to act as a 'toll booth' on this critical shipping lane complicates American military and diplomatic options.

Despite Trump's prime-time push to rally public support for the war effort, financial markets have remained largely unmoved, reflecting investor uncertainty about the conflict's trajectory. Critics argue the escalating war and rising gas prices are taking a political toll on the administration, while supporters contend decisive military action is necessary to neutralise a long-standing threat.

sources.title: Trump: US military ‘hasn’t even started destroying what’s left in Iran’
Iran hits Gulf refineries as Trump warns U.S. will attack Iranian bridges, power plants
Iran ‘toll booth’ on Strait of Hormuz underscores its leverage on Trump, US

What B-52 bombers bring to Iran fight — and what it means for the war now
WATCH: President Trump reveals families of slain US service members urged him ‘finish the job’
Evacuation of U.S. troops from Mideast base sends community groups scrambling to help
USS Gerald R. Ford aircraft carrier to return to Middle East amid Iran fight

us-politics.headline: Pam Bondi Fired as Attorney General, Raising DOJ and Epstein Questions
us-politics.body:
President Trump has dismissed Attorney General Pam Bondi, triggering significant questions about the future direction of the Department of Justice. Analysts suggest Bondi's failure to deliver high-profile prosecutions — the political 'scalps' Trump reportedly sought — ultimately sealed her fate. The shake-up leaves at least five major questions unanswered, including the DOJ's ongoing cases, its relationship with other federal agencies, and the broader politicisation of the department.

One of the most prominent concerns following Bondi's departure is the fate of the Jeffrey Epstein inquiry. Despite the release of Epstein-related files, few arrests have materialised, frustrating lawmakers on both sides of the aisle. Democratic Representative Ro Khanna has publicly questioned whether the investigation will continue with the same vigour under new leadership, calling for transparency and accountability.

Separately, the DOJ's plan to share state voter data with the Department of Homeland Security has drawn fresh scrutiny after a key privacy officer resigned in protest. Critics warn the data-sharing arrangement raises serious civil liberties concerns, while the administration argues it is necessary to ensure election integrity. The cumulative effect of these controversies has placed the DOJ at the centre of intense political debate.

sources.title: Whole Hog Politics: Bondi beached
Rep. Ro Khanna, D-Calif., on the future of the Epstein inquiry with Bondi's ousting
Five questions hanging over the DOJ shake-up
The Memo: Bondi’s failure to get Trump his ‘scalps’ sealed her fate
After the release of the Epstein files, why have there been so few arrests?
As DOJ prepares to share state voter data with DHS, a key privacy officer resigns

us-politics.headline: 2028 Republican Race Takes Shape Amid Trump Administration Turbulence
us-politics.body:
With the 2028 presidential race still years away, early positioning within the Republican Party is already generating significant commentary. Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio are seen as the leading contenders for the GOP nomination, with analysts examining how each is navigating the unpredictability of the Trump era. Vance's upcoming meeting with Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán ahead of Hungary's parliamentary elections has drawn attention, with supporters framing it as proactive diplomacy and critics questioning the optics of engaging with a controversial European leader.

On the domestic political front, Trump's re-endorsement of Republican Congressman Jeff Hurd — after briefly withdrawing support — illustrates the volatile nature of the president's political alliances. Meanwhile, Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt has been defending the SAVE America Act at conservative events, signalling the administration's continued focus on its core legislative priorities.

In Maine, Democratic political outsider Graham Platner is gaining traction in the Senate race, reflecting broader Democratic efforts to rebuild in competitive states. Democrats are also navigating an uncomfortable situation over campaign donations linked to data firm Palantir, which has contracts tied to immigration enforcement — a tension that highlights the complex fundraising pressures facing the party heading into the midterms.

sources.title: The Vance versus Rubio 2028 nomination fight through the lens of chaos
VP Vance to meet with Viktor Orbán in Hungary days ahead of foreign nation's elections
Graham Platner’s political outsider vibe gains steam in Maine among Democrats
Palantir-linked campaign donations put Democrats in tight spot
How Trump came to re-endorse a Republican congressman he had just ditched
Karoline Leavitt reveals 'anti-climatic' way Trump told her she’d be press secretary: ‘Oh, by the way’
Leavitt defends SAVE America Act push at Turning Point event with Erika Kirk

us-politics.headline: Domestic Policy Battles Span Race, Unions, Drug Tariffs, and Civil Liberties
us-politics.body:
A range of domestic policy disputes are unfolding simultaneously across the country. A new lawsuit alleges that the Congressional Black Caucus Foundation's scholarship programme discriminates on the basis of race, a claim the foundation is expected to contest vigorously. The case touches on longstanding debates about race-conscious programmes and equal protection under the law, with legal observers watching closely given the current composition of the Supreme Court.

On labour policy, contrasting approaches in Florida and Oregon are drawing comparisons. Florida's recent legislation has imposed stricter accountability measures on public-sector unions, while Oregon has moved in the opposite direction by expanding union protections — reflecting a deepening national divide over the role of organised labour in government.

President Trump has announced tariffs of up to 100% on some brand-name drug imports as part of an 'America First' push aimed at boosting domestic pharmaceutical manufacturing, a move supporters say will reduce foreign dependency while critics warn it could raise prescription costs. Separately, advocacy groups have raised alarm over a Trump administration demand for a list of Jewish faculty at the University of Pennsylvania as part of an antisemitism probe, with civil liberties organisations calling it 'incredibly chilling.' Republicans in Congress are also pushing to defund NIH research they characterise as transgender-related animal testing, adding another front to ongoing culture war battles.

sources.title: Black Caucus scholarships discriminate based on race, lawsuit alleges
Florida made public-sector unions more accountable — Oregon did the opposite
‘Incredibly chilling’: Advocacy groups raise alarm over Trump’s demand for list of Jewish faculty at Penn
GOP targets transgender animal testing in defund demand to NIH
Takeaways from Trump's tough week, as war and gas prices take a toll
Colorado House advances conversion therapy lawsuit bill; GOP lawmaker calls it ‘slap in the face’ to SCOTUS
Trump slaps up to 100% tariff on some brand-name drug imports in major America First push

au-politics.headline: Australia's Fuel Security Crisis Deepens Amid Industry and Political Concern
au-politics.body:
Australia's fuel security situation has drawn significant attention, with industry representatives warning that fuel stocks heading to Australia would not last a month. The concern centres on the adequacy of reserves and the vulnerability of supply chains, particularly in the context of broader regional instability.

The Guardian has reported on the human impact of Asia's wider fuel crisis, illustrating how ordinary workers and communities across the region are feeling the strain of tightening supply. The crisis has roots in geopolitical tensions affecting oil-producing nations, raising questions about the long-term reliability of fuel imports for countries like Australia.

Politicians have put forward several proposals to address the shortfall, though analysts have questioned the effectiveness of the most prominent ideas being floated, describing some as inadequate or misdirected. Energy Minister Chris Bowen has faced scrutiny over the government's response, with industry stakeholders calling for more decisive action. The debate reflects a broader tension between Australia's net-zero commitments and its continued dependence on fossil fuel imports, a contradiction that commentators across the political spectrum have noted with varying degrees of urgency.

sources.title: Fuel heading to Australia won't last a month, industry says
A day in the life of Asia’s fuel crisis
‘Not a fix’: the three questionable ideas politicians are pitching to solve Australia’s fuel crisis

au-politics.headline: Easter Long Weekend: Celebrations, Fatal Crashes, and Community Gatherings Across Australia
au-politics.body:
Australians marked Good Friday with a mix of religious observance, seafood traditions, and community events. The newly opened Sydney Fish Market drew large crowds during the Easter rush, with shoppers embracing the upgraded facility. Church services and local boat races also featured prominently in how communities observed the public holiday across the country.

However, the long weekend was also marked by tragedy on Australian roads. Three people, including a 14-year-old dirt bike rider, died in separate crashes in Western Australia, while a motorcyclist was killed in a traffic light collision in Adelaide and another died in a crash on the Monaro Highway in southern New South Wales. Authorities have urged motorists to exercise caution over the remainder of the Easter period.

On the sporting front, the AFL and NRL provided entertainment for the holiday weekend. Fremantle claimed a thriller against Adelaide after a Crows player hit the post in the dying seconds, while the Melbourne Storm suffered a rare heavy defeat to the Penrith Panthers — their first time conceding 50 points since 2003 — in a result that surprised both clubs and commentators alike.

sources.title: Highway closed after motorcyclist dies in crash in southern NSW
Packed to the gills: shoppers embrace new Sydney Fish Market during Good Friday rush
Dirt bike rider, 14, among three dead in separate WA Easter long weekend crashes
Dockers claim thriller after Crow hits post inside final minute
Australians mark Good Friday with seafood, church services and a boat race
Motorcyclist killed in traffic light crash in Adelaide, another rushed to hospital

au-politics.headline: Trump Administration Shake-Up and US Policy Moves Draw International Scrutiny
au-politics.body:
The Trump administration has continued to generate significant international attention, with a wave of high-profile dismissals prompting analysis of the president's governing style. Among those removed was Attorney-General Pam Bondi, with observers noting that several of Trump's most prominent female supporters have found themselves sidelined. Commentators have described the pattern as reflecting an increasingly centralised and unpredictable White House.

Trump's recent speech regarding Iran has also drawn sharp criticism. A former US envoy involved in the Iran nuclear deal described the address as "delusional," raising concerns about the administration's approach to diplomacy with Tehran at a time of heightened regional tension. The remarks have added to international unease about US foreign policy direction.

Separately, Trump's announcement of a 100% tariff on pharmaceuticals imported into the United States has had implications for Australia. The Australian government has moved to reassure the public that domestic drug prices will not rise as a result of the tariff, though the policy has intensified debate about the resilience of global supply chains and the potential flow-on effects of US trade decisions for allied nations.

sources.title: Donald Trump is going on a firing spree
Trump's fiercest female defenders are now on the outer
Australia says it won’t raise drug prices after Trump’s 100% tariff on pharmaceuticals imported into US
'Every word is delusional': Trump's speech lashed by former US envoy to Iran

au-politics.headline: Australian Security and Justice: Terror Charges, Child Abuse Sentencing, and Fuel Reserves
au-politics.body:
Two New South Wales teenagers have been charged over violent extremist material allegedly linked to terrorism, in a case that has highlighted ongoing concerns about radicalisation among young people. Police allege the material was connected to terror-related activity, and the charges have prompted renewed discussion about how authorities monitor and respond to extremist content online.

In Queensland, a North Lakes man has been sentenced to 15 years in prison after being convicted of live-streaming child sexual abuse. The case drew widespread condemnation and has reignited calls for stronger protections for children in online environments, as well as greater international cooperation to combat the distribution of abuse material across digital platforms.

On environmental matters, a plan to protect Western Australia's Fitzroy River — the state's largest — has been released, though some conservationists and community groups have questioned whether the measures go far enough. The Fitzroy is of significant cultural importance to First Nations communities in the Kimberley region, and debate continues over how to balance development interests with the river's ecological and cultural protection.

sources.title: Queensland man jailed for 15 years after live-streaming child abuse
Does a 'historic' plan to protect WA's largest river go far enough?
Two NSW teenagers charged over violent extremist material allegedly linked to terror

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Escalates Amid War Crimes Allegations and Regional Strikes
world.body:
Now in its 35th day, the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran continues to intensify, with strikes hitting civilian infrastructure including a bridge in Karaj and sites across the country. Iran has condemned what it calls a 'moral collapse' by the US and Israel, while Iranians on the ground express deep fear about what further escalation may bring.

Over 100 American legal experts have signed a statement condemning the strikes as potential war crimes, alleging serious violations of international law. The condemnation adds significant pressure on the Trump administration, which has offered shifting and unclear timelines for how and when the conflict might conclude.

The war's regional spillover is becoming increasingly apparent. Kuwait has reported missile and drone strikes on a desalination plant and oil refinery, raising alarm about attacks on critical civilian infrastructure beyond Iran's borders. Meanwhile, China's independent 'teapot' oil refineries are helping Beijing weather the resulting global oil supply disruptions, highlighting the conflict's far-reaching economic consequences.

sources.title: International law experts allege violations in Iran war
When will the Iran war end? Tracing the Trump administration's timelines
Over 100 US legal experts condemn strikes on Iran as possible ‘war crimes’
'This has got me worried': Iranians fear what comes next after US strike on Karaj bridge
Kuwait desalination plant, oil refinery hit by missile and drone strikes
Iran condemns US-Israeli ‘moral collapse’ after attacks on civilian sites
Iran war: What is happening on day 35 of US-Israeli attacks?
How China’s ‘teapot’ refineries are cushioning it from Iran war oil crisis

world.headline: Trump Shakes Up US Government: Bondi Fired, Army Leadership Overhauled
world.body:
President Trump has removed Attorney General Pam Bondi from the Justice Department, replacing her with Todd Blanche. The dismissal is the latest in a series of high-profile personnel changes under the Trump administration, raising questions about the independence of the Justice Department and the motivations behind the reshuffle.

Simultaneously, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has fired the US Army Chief of Staff as part of a reported broader string of military leadership dismissals. General Christopher LaNeve has been named to lead the US Army, stepping into a role of significant responsibility amid an active overseas conflict. The back-to-back removals of senior civilian and military officials signal an aggressive consolidation of loyalty-driven leadership across key institutions.

The cumulative effect of these changes is drawing scrutiny from lawmakers and analysts who warn that hollowing out experienced leadership during an active war could have serious operational consequences.

sources.title: Who is Christopher LaNeve, set to lead the US army?
Trump removes US Attorney General Pam Bondi
Why did Trump fire Pam Bondi from Justice Department, who is Todd Blanche?
Hegseth fires US Army chief of staff in reported string of dismissals

world.headline: Artemis II Reaches Far Side of Moon in Historic Crewed Mission
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II mission has reached a major milestone, with the spacecraft leaving Earth's orbit and successfully firing its engines to set course for the far side of the Moon. The mission marks the first time humans have traveled beyond Earth's orbit in over five decades, representing a landmark moment for human space exploration.

The four-person crew has spoken publicly from space, describing the experience and the significance of the journey. The mission is designed as a crewed flyby rather than a lunar landing, serving as a critical test of the Orion spacecraft and Space Launch System ahead of future Moon landing attempts planned under the broader Artemis program.

The successful progression of Artemis II comes as a rare piece of positive international news, with scientists and space agencies around the world watching closely as humanity takes its next steps toward a sustained presence beyond Earth.

sources.title: Artemis II leaves Earth's orbit on track for far side of the Moon
Artemis II mission fires up engines in next step of moon mission
Watch: Artemis II astronauts speak from space

world.headline: Myanmar Coup Leader Becomes President; Burkina Faso Rejects Democracy
world.body:
Myanmar's military coup leader, Min Aung Hlaing, has been formally elected president by the country's pro-military parliament, cementing his grip on power more than four years after he led the coup that ousted the elected civilian government. The move is widely seen as an attempt to legitimize military rule amid an ongoing and brutal civil war that has devastated the country.

In a separate but thematically resonant development, Burkina Faso's military leader Ibrahim Traoré has publicly declared that his country should 'forget' about democracy, doubling down on the junta's authoritarian trajectory since seizing power. The statement signals a deepening rejection of democratic governance across parts of West Africa and Southeast Asia where military governments have taken hold.

Together, the developments reflect a troubling global trend of military leaders entrenching themselves in power and openly dismissing democratic norms, drawing condemnation from international observers and human rights organizations.

sources.title: Burkina Faso military leader Traore says ‘forget democracy’
Burkina Faso must 'forget' about democracy, military leader says
Myanmar's coup leader who set off a brutal civil war becomes president
Myanmar’s coup leader elected president by pro-military parliament

ai.headline: OpenAI Acquires Media Property as AI Industry Raises Fresh Ethical Concerns
ai.body:
OpenAI has acquired TBPN, a founder-focused business talk show with a significant following in tech circles. Critics are framing the move as a strategic purchase of favorable media coverage rather than a content play, raising questions about the blurring line between AI companies and the press that covers them.

Meanwhile, AI is pushing into sensitive new territory. Chatbots are now being used to prescribe and refill psychiatric medications, a development that has alarmed mental health professionals and patient advocates who question whether AI systems are equipped to handle the nuance and risk involved in psychiatric care.

Privacy concerns are also mounting around AI-adjacent tools. The note-taking app Granola was found to make user notes publicly accessible to anyone with a link by default, exposing potentially sensitive meeting content. Separately, Perplexity is facing a lawsuit alleging its "Incognito Mode" does not actually protect user privacy as advertised, with plaintiffs calling it a sham. Together, these stories reflect growing scrutiny of how AI and tech companies handle user trust and data.

sources.title: Chatbots are now prescribing psychiatric drugs
PSA: Anyone with a link can view your Granola notes by default
Perplexity's "Incognito Mode" is a "sham," lawsuit says
OpenAI Acquires Tech Talk Show ‘TBPN’—and Buys Itself Some Positive News
OpenAI acquires TBPN, the buzzy founder-led business talk show
OpenAI just bought TBPN

ai.headline: AI Tools Expand Rapidly While Security and Infrastructure Risks Grow
ai.body:
The AI development ecosystem continues to accelerate. Cursor released version 3 of its AI-powered coding environment, while Google upgraded its Vids platform with Veo and Lyria generative models and directable AI avatars, signaling a push into AI-generated video for workplace productivity. On the hobbyist side, developers are sharing guides for running large models like Gemma 4 26B locally on Apple hardware using Ollama, and a new Mac app called Apfel aims to make free local AI more accessible.

However, infrastructure risks are emerging alongside this growth. Researchers have disclosed new Rowhammer attacks that can give attackers complete control of machines running Nvidia GPUs — hardware that underpins much of the AI industry's compute stack. The vulnerability is particularly concerning given how widely Nvidia GPUs are deployed in data centers.

On the energy front, Google is funding a new data center that will be powered by a large natural gas plant, reigniting debate about the environmental cost of AI infrastructure. Switzerland, meanwhile, is positioning itself as a global hub for semiconductor research, with a new facility being compared in ambition to CERN.

sources.title: April 2026 TLDR Setup for Ollama and Gemma 4 26B on a Mac mini
Show HN: Apfel – The free AI already on your Mac
New Rowhammer attacks give complete control of machines running Nvidia GPUs
Google Vids gets AI upgrade with Veo and Lyria models, directable AI avatars
A New Google-Funded Data Center Will Be Powered by a Massive Gas Plant
Cursor 3
Switzerland hosts 'CERN of semiconductor research'

ai.headline: EU Digital Sovereignty Under Pressure as Tech Policy Battles Intensify
ai.body:
The European Union is facing sharp criticism after reportedly softening its digital regulations under pressure from the United States. Observers and advocacy groups have called the move a "fatal decision," arguing it undermines the EU's ability to set independent standards for the digital economy and protect European citizens from the dominance of large American tech platforms.

The backlash has fueled interest in European-built alternatives to US tech services. A new resource cataloguing EU-based substitutes for tools like Google, Apple services, and Dropbox has gained traction, reflecting a broader desire among European users and institutions to reduce dependence on American platforms amid ongoing geopolitical and regulatory tensions.

In the UK, a separate but related controversy is unfolding within the NHS. Staff are reportedly refusing to use the Federated Data Platform due to ethical concerns about its operator, Palantir — the US data analytics firm with deep ties to government surveillance and defense contracts. The resistance highlights how distrust of specific technology vendors is becoming a practical operational challenge for public institutions, not merely an abstract policy debate.

sources.title: 'Fatal decision': EU slammed for caving to US pressure on digital rules
Show HN: European alternatives to Google, Apple, Dropbox and 120 US apps
NHS staff refusing to use FDP over Palantir ethical concerns

ai.headline: Space Race Heats Up With Moon Mission Launch and Starlink Satellite Dispute
ai.body:
Four astronauts have departed for the Moon in what marks a significant milestone for human deep space exploration. The mission, covered by Ars Technica, represents a major step in ongoing efforts to return humans to the lunar surface, with the crew now committed to their trajectory.

Back in low Earth orbit, a regulatory and safety dispute is escalating between SpaceX and Amazon. SpaceX has filed arguments with the FCC claiming that Amazon's Project Kuiper satellites have been placed at incorrect altitudes, raising the risk of collisions with Starlink's existing constellation. The complaint underscores the growing congestion in low Earth orbit as multiple commercial operators race to build out satellite internet networks, and highlights the need for clearer international coordination on orbital traffic management.

The competing claims reflect broader tensions in the commercial space industry, where SpaceX's dominant position is increasingly being challenged by well-funded rivals. The FCC will need to adjudicate technical and safety claims that have significant commercial implications for both companies.

sources.title: Four astronauts are now inexorably bound for the Moon
SpaceX tries to convince FCC that Amazon put satellites into wrong altitude

btc.headline: Bitcoin Enters Holiday Weekend Under Pressure With Bearish Signals Mounting
btc.body:
Bitcoin is heading into the Good Friday holiday weekend in a vulnerable position, with key institutional flow mechanisms — including ETF trading and CME futures — going offline at a sensitive moment. Futures markets have tilted bearish, volatility has cooled, and crypto broadly appears to be consolidating rather than recovering.

On-chain data adds to the cautious outlook: the share of Bitcoin supply held in profit has dropped to levels analysts associate with a "true bear market," suggesting significant portions of the holder base are underwater. Miner behavior reflects this pressure — Riot Platforms sold 3,778 BTC during Q1, signaling that even major miners are liquidating holdings rather than accumulating.

Against this backdrop, ARK Invest's Cathie Wood has offered a more optimistic long-term view, arguing that Bitcoin is "done" with the extreme 85% drawdowns that characterized earlier cycles, and pointing to a $34,000 price target. However, with macro factors like oil prices and broader risk-off sentiment stirring, near-term sentiment remains subdued as markets await clearer directional catalysts after the holiday break.

sources.title: Bitcoin 'done' with 85% crashes, says Cathie Wood amid new $34K target
Crypto snoozes into Good Friday as oil and macro stir: Crypto Daybook Americas
Crypto consolidates as volatility cools and futures markets tilt bearish
Bitcoin supply in profit heads to ‘true bear market’ levels
Bitcoin heads into holiday weekend exposed as ETF and CME flows go offline

btc.headline: DeFi Security Under Scrutiny as Hackers Steal $169M and Drift Pursues Exploiter
btc.body:
Decentralized finance suffered $169 million in losses across 34 protocol exploits during the first quarter of 2026, according to DefiLlama data — though the figure represents a decline from prior periods, underscoring that security remains a persistent structural challenge for the sector.

In one notable ongoing case, Drift Protocol sent an on-chain message directly to wallets linked to a $280 million exploit, attempting to open dialogue with the attacker. The move highlights an increasingly common tactic in DeFi incident response, where protocols use blockchain's transparent messaging layer to negotiate or apply public pressure on bad actors.

A separate Bank of Canada study on Aave V3 offered nuanced findings: the lending protocol successfully avoided accumulating bad debt during periods of market stress, but did so partly by shifting liquidation risk onto borrowers, who absorbed significant losses in the process. The research raises important questions about how DeFi risk is distributed and whether protocol-level stability metrics tell the full story of user outcomes during volatile conditions.

sources.title: Aave avoided bad debt by shifting risk to borrowers: Bank of Canada study
Drift sends onchain message to wallets tied to $280M exploit
Crypto hackers steal $169M from 34 DeFi protocols in Q1: DefiLlama

btc.headline: Stablecoins, Tokenization, and Wrapped Bitcoin Signal Maturing Crypto Infrastructure
btc.body:
Stablecoins surpassed the monthly transaction volume of the Automated Clearing House network in February, a milestone that underscores their growing role as a genuine payments infrastructure rather than a purely speculative instrument. The ACH network processes trillions in U.S. bank transfers annually, making the comparison a meaningful benchmark for stablecoin adoption.

Circle is moving to capitalize on institutional momentum by launching cirBTC, a wrapped Bitcoin token designed to compete directly with offerings from BitGo and Coinbase. The product targets institutional markets seeking regulated, compliant exposure to Bitcoin within DeFi and tokenized finance environments.

The IMF weighed in on the broader tokenization trend, acknowledging that converting real-world assets onto blockchains improves financial efficiency and accessibility, but warning that it also introduces new risks around liquidity, interconnectedness, and regulatory gaps. Meanwhile, the Ethereum Foundation completed its staking of 70,000 ETH — worth approximately $93 million — signaling institutional confidence in Ethereum's proof-of-stake infrastructure even as quantum computing threats loom. Naoris Protocol launched a quantum-resistant blockchain in direct response to so-called "Q-Day" risks facing Bitcoin and Ethereum.

sources.title: Ethereum Foundation stakes another $93 million ether, reaching its 70,000 ETH target
Naoris Protocol's quantum-resistant blockchain goes live as Bitcoin and Ethereum face 'Q-Day' threats
Tokenization makes finance more efficient but introduces risks: IMF
Stablecoins flip automated clearing house volume in February
Circle to launch cirBTC wrapped Bitcoin, challenging BitGo and Coinbase

btc.headline: Regulatory and Institutional Shifts Reshape Crypto's Political and Market Landscape
btc.body:
Todd Blanche, the architect of the Department of Justice's crypto enforcement memo that significantly narrowed the scope of federal cryptocurrency prosecutions, has been named interim U.S. Attorney General. The appointment is likely to have lasting implications for how federal law enforcement approaches digital asset cases, potentially reinforcing the more restrained enforcement posture outlined in his earlier memo.

In South Korea, traditional finance is continuing its push into crypto: Korea Investment & Securities is reportedly exploring an acquisition of a stake in Coinone, one of the country's major exchanges. The move reflects a broader global trend of established brokerages and financial institutions seeking direct footholds in regulated crypto trading venues.

On the technology and AI frontier, major Big Tech firms have backed the newly formed x402 Foundation, aimed at advancing the adoption of agentic AI through blockchain-based payment protocols. The initiative signals growing convergence between artificial intelligence infrastructure and crypto payment rails, with the x402 protocol designed to enable autonomous AI agents to transact on-chain — a development that could open significant new use cases for blockchain networks.

sources.title: South Korean brokerage Korea Investment & Securities eyes Coinone stake: Report
Big Tech firms back new x402 Foundation to advance agentic AI adoption
Todd Blanche, author of DOJ crypto enforcement memo, is now interim AG

food.name: Manti (Turkish Lamb Dumplings with Yogurt & Chilli Butter)
food.description: Tiny hand-pinched dumplings filled with spiced minced lamb, drowned in garlicky yogurt and a sizzling paprika-butter sauce — a dish where the contrast of hot and cold, rich and tangy, is the entire point.
food.tip: Make the dumplings as small as you can manage — tradition holds that a good cook fits 40 in a single spoon, and the smaller they are, the better the sauce-to-filling ratio.

exercise.name: Friday Kettlebell Flow
exercise.equipment: One kettlebell (12–20 kg depending on level)
exercise.description: A flowing circuit to close out the week: 5 rounds of 10 swings, 5 clean-and-press per side, 8 goblet squats, and 10 single-leg Romanian deadlifts per side. Rest 90 seconds between rounds. Finisher: 3 minutes of continuous swings at a conversational pace. Emphasis on hip hinge mechanics and controlled breathing throughout.

learn.topic: The Antikythera Mechanism — the world's first analogue computer
learn.description: Recovered from a Roman-era shipwreck off the Greek island of Antikythera in 1901, this 2,100-year-old bronze device could predict solar and lunar eclipses, track the positions of the five known planets, and calculate the four-year Olympic cycle — all via an intricate system of at least 30 meshing gears. Recent X-ray tomography by the Antikythera Research Team has revealed new inscriptions and gear arrangements that scholars are still decoding.
learn.why: A 2024–2025 wave of new imaging analysis has reopened debate about who built it and whether a second, companion device existed — making this ancient mystery feel startlingly alive right now, and a perfect Friday rabbit hole.
